symbols: skull beneath his hand — memento mori, meditation on death and the transience of earthly life
red cloak — Jerome's cardinal dignity in later tradition, or the passion of penitential devotion
emaciated body — the ascetic discipline of fasting and self-denial in pursuit of holiness
book or manuscript — Jerome's monumental work of translating the Bible into Latin (the Vulgate)
description: A painting of St. Jerome depicted as an elderly, gaunt man with a long white beard and bald head, seated in a rocky setting. He wears a voluminous red cloak draped over his thin, bare-chested body. His right hand rests on or near a human skull placed on a stone ledge, while he gazes downward in contemplation. The background suggests a cave or rocky wilderness. The figure is powerfully lit from the left, highlighting his aged, ascetic physique against the shadowed background.
theological_description: St. Jerome (c. 347-420 AD) was one of the greatest Church Fathers, best known for translating the Bible into Latin (the Vulgate), which remained the standard text of Western Christianity for over a millennium. While a post-biblical figure, Jerome's life was consumed by Scripture. He famously declared 'Ignorance of Scripture is ignorance of Christ,' echoing the principle of 2 Timothy 3:16-17 that all Scripture is God-breathed and profitable. His penitential posture in the wilderness reflects the biblical tradition of desert spirituality — Moses on Sinai, Elijah at Horeb, Jesus's forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-2). The skull he contemplates embodies Ecclesiastes 12:7: 'The dust returns to the ground it came from, and the spirit returns to God who gave it.' Jerome's gaunt asceticism reflects Paul's discipline in 1 Corinthians 9:27: 'I discipline my body and keep it under control.' His lifelong devotion to the sacred text fulfills the psalmist's prayer: 'Open my eyes that I may see wonderful things in your law' (Psalm 119:18).

scripture_refs.reason: All Scripture is God-breathed — the conviction that drove Jerome's monumental translation work
Dust returns to the ground — the skull meditation on mortality rooted in Ecclesiastes
Jesus's wilderness retreat as the model for Jerome's desert asceticism and penitence
Open my eyes to see wonderful things in your law — Jerome's lifelong devotion to understanding Scripture
